steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence has one subject — “A recent study” — and needs a main verb. The blank must be a finite (conjugated) verb that can serve as the predicate of an independent clause; a non-finite form would leave the clause without a true verb.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘explains’: a finite present-tense verb that agrees with the singular subject “A recent study” and serves as the main predicate; the clause is grammatically complete. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘explaining’: a present participle (non-finite); it cannot be the main verb of a clause and creates a sentence fragment. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘having explained’: a perfect participle (non-finite); like Choice B, it cannot anchor an independent clause and leaves the sentence without a predicate. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘to explain’: an infinitive (non-finite); infinitives cannot function as the main verb of a clause and produce a sentence fragment. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
